title: Loss-induced nonreciprocal quantum battery
abstract: Nonreciprocal quantum batteries offer superior charging performance compared to reciprocal quantum batteries. We consider a charger-battery system comprising two optical cavities that interact independently with a third auxiliary cavity. We show that the nonzero dissipation of the auxiliary cavity induces a nonreciprocal exchange of excitations among the charger-battery system. Therefore, by engineering the loss in the auxiliary cavity, we induce a directional energy flow that enhances the charging efficiency. Using numerical and analytical calculations, we show that the steady-state energy stored in the battery significantly exceeds that in the charger. We compare our results with those of the reciprocal cases and demonstrate that our nonreciprocal quantum battery model exhibits a significant charging advantage. We believe that our proposed scheme represents a step forward in cavity-loss engineering, making it a viable approach for nonreciprocal quantum batteries with existing experimental techniques.
summary: The paper proposes a method to enhance quantum battery charging by using an auxiliary cavity with controlled loss to create nonreciprocal energy flow. This mechanism suppresses energy flowing back from the battery to the charger, significantly increasing the steady-state energy storage capacity. The approach is potentially implementable using existing cavity QED or circuit QED techniques.
main_problem: Improving the charging efficiency and performance of quantum batteries by developing a nonreciprocal model that achieves directional energy flow.
main_result: By engineering the loss in an auxiliary cavity, the authors induce a nonreciprocal energy flow that suppresses back-transmission, achieving a steady-state energy storage advantage of up to 8 times compared to bipartite systems.
method: The study uses a combination of analytical derivations via quantum Langevin equations and input-output formalism, alongside numerical simulations using the QuTiP software package.
model_or_system: A three-cavity system consisting of a charger cavity, a battery cavity, and an auxiliary cavity, modeled using a tripartite bosonic Hamiltonian and a Lindblad master equation to account for local decay rates.
key_observables: Energy stored in the battery and charger, energy transfer gain (ratio of battery to charger energy), and transmission coefficients.
important_parameters: Decay rates (kappa), coupling strengths (J), driving amplitude (Omega), detuning (Delta), and the phase of the coupling (theta).
main_assumptions: The system follows Markovian dynamics, operates in the weak drive limit, and assumes all stored energy can be extracted (ergotropy equals total energy).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the three-cavity schematic; Figure 2 displays the time evolution of energy in the charger and battery and the evolution of energy transfer gain; Figure 3 shows steady-state gain versus coupling strength.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantum battery efficiency, describes the three-cavity model and its Hamiltonian, provides analytical derivations for nonreciprocal transmission, presents numerical results comparing the proposed model to reciprocal and bipartite cases, and concludes with experimental implementation suggestions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it demonstrates how engineered dissipation (loss engineering) can be used as a resource to break reciprocity and enhance energy transfer.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. This figure illustrates the schematic of the model containing three cavities, ˆa, ˆb, and ˆc, having decay rates κa, κb, and κc, respectively. Here, cavity ˆa serves as charger, cavity ˆc as battery and an additional cavity ˆb is introduced to induce nonreciprocal transmission from cavity ˆa to ˆc by engineering local dephasing of cavity ˆb.

FIG. 2. (a) Energy stored in the charger and battery plotted against the scaled time Jt. At t = 0, both the charger and the battery are in their ground states. In a steady state, the total energy of the battery surpasses that of the charger because of nonreciprocity in the system. (b) This plot illustrates the ratio of energy stored in the battery to the charger versus the scaled time Jt. Here, ∆a = ∆b = ∆c = ∆= 0, Jab = Jac = Jbc = J = √
FIG. 4. ηss ac is plotted against κb for different values of θ, rest of the parameters are J = κ, κa = κc = κ. As κb →∞, ηss ac →4J2/κ2 c.

FIG. 3. Steady state value of energy transfer gain ηss ac plotted against various system parameters. While rest of the param- eters are as follows: (a) θ = −π/4 (b) κb = 40κ (c) J = 4κ (d) J = 4κ and θ = −π/2; in all subplots Ω= 0.1κ and κa = κc = κ

FIG. 5. (a) Energy stored in reciprocal charger and battery system versus scaled time. One can observe that after the transient phase, the energy of both the charger and battery converges to the same value. (b) Energy ratio of both battery and charger as a function of scaled time converges to 1 in the steady state. We used the same set of parameters as in the Fig. 2 except κb = 0.
